serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate serving of approximately 400 g, as typically portioned by GrabFood vendors in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Tofu, stir-fried (FDC ID 172476) and mixed stir-fried vegetable dish analogues; cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition of Food database for Thai stir-fry dishes

nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for 'Vegetarian Stir-fried Thai-style Holy Basil (Pad Ka Prao Jay)' exists in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore database. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for stir-fried tofu and mixed vegetable dishes (FDC ID 172476 and related entries) and HPB Singapore data for Thai stir-fry analogues. The input record carried ai_generated provenance with confidence 0.7 and listed 'grabfood' as data_source, which is a forbidden primary source under Project Veritas v2 source policy. Input values have been retained only where consistent with USDA FoodData Central and HPB Singapore analogues. All values are treated as estimated.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; University of Sydney GI Database analogues for stir-fried rice and noodle dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vegetarian Pad Ka Prao Jay. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on analogous stir-fried noodle and rice dish entries from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The dish is assumed to be served with or contain jasmine rice (GI ~65–70) or rice noodles (GI ~61) as the carbohydrate base. A blended GI of 65 is used as a conservative medium estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 65.0) / 100 = 42.3 (serving = 400 g; carbohydrate = 16.25 g per 100 g × 4 = 65.0 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 375 mg per 100 g from soy sauce and vegetarian oyster sauce; estimated 1500 mg sodium per standard 400 g plate
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and mushroom sauce, limit portion to half a plate, and pair with potassium-rich foods such as banana or spinach to partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 16.25 g per 100 g with only 1.75 g dietary fibre; medium GI of 65 and high glycemic load of 42.3 per standard 400 g plate
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should request a smaller rice or noodle portion, increase the tofu and vegetable ratio, and ask for sauce on the side to reduce sugar and sodium intake and blunt post-meal glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is low at 1.0 g per 100 g; cholesterol is 0 mg; trans fat is negligible at 0.03 g per 100 g; however sodium at 375 mg per 100 g is a secondary concern
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The lipid profile of this dish is favourable for cardiovascular health given low saturated fat and zero cholesterol, but the high sodium content (estimated 1500 mg per plate) warrants portion control and sauce reduction for individuals managing cardiovascular disease.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Holy basil provides anti-inflammatory polyphenols; vitamin C at 10 mg per 100 g from bell peppers; low saturated fat; however nightshade vegetables (bell pepper, chili) may aggravate solanine-sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This dish is generally suitable for rheumatoid arthritis due to its anti-inflammatory herb content and low saturated fat, but individuals sensitive to nightshade solanine should request reduced chili and bell pepper, and should limit sodium by requesting reduced sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetarian Stir-fried Thai-style Holy Basil (Pad Ka Prao Jay) is a plant-based adaptation of the classic Thai street-food dish Pad Ka Prao, prepared without meat by substituting tofu or mixed mushrooms as the protein source. The dish is stir-fried at high heat in a wok with garlic, fresh chili, holy basil leaves (ka prao), bell peppers, and mixed vegetables, seasoned with vegetarian oyster sauce (mushroom-based), light soy sauce, and a small amount of sugar. It is typically served over jasmine rice or rice noodles. The flavour profile is savoury, slightly spicy, and fragrant from the holy basil, with a tender-crisp texture from the vegetables and a soft, lightly browned exterior on the tofu. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 137.5 kcal, 4.5 g protein, 6.25 g fat (saturated fat 1.0 g), 16.25 g carbohydrate, 1.75 g dietary fibre, 375 mg sodium, 37.5 mg calcium, 0.75 mg iron, and 10 mg vitamin C. The glycemic index is estimated at 65 (medium), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 42.3 per standard 400 g plate. Clinically, the dish is moderate concern for hypertension and Type 2 Diabetes due to its sodium content and medium-GI carbohydrate base, but is low concern for cardiovascular disease given its negligible saturated fat and zero cholesterol. Holy basil contributes anti-inflammatory polyphenols relevant to rheumatoid arthritis management. This dish is popular in Singapore's Thai food stalls and GrabFood delivery menus, and is suitable for vegetarians.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for this dish exists in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore database. Nutrient values are adapted from the input record (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) cross-referenced against USDA stir-fried tofu and vegetable analogues and HPB Thai stir-fry data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium because the values are internally consistent and plausible for the dish type, but are not directly sourced from a primary approved database entry for this specific dish.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific vegetarian Thai stir-fry dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore database as of 2024. 2. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero — this is a fully plant-based dish containing no animal-derived ingredients; cholesterol is naturally zero per USDA FoodData Central stir-fried tofu analogues. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.03 g per 100 g: trace level from vegetable oil used in stir-frying; consistent with USDA data for stir-fried vegetable dishes. 4. The input data_source was 'grabfood', which is a forbidden primary source under Project Veritas v2 source policy. Nutrient values have been retained only where consistent with USDA FoodData Central and HPB Singapore analogues. 5. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on jasmine rice and rice noodle GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). 6. The dish name '609D' is a menu item identifier from GrabFood; the canonical food name has been normalised to 'Vegetarian Stir-fried Thai-style Holy Basil (Pad Ka Prao Jay)'. 7. Vegan flag set to false as vegetarian oyster sauce (mushroom-based) may contain trace non-vegan additives depending on brand; halal status unconfirmed as soy sauce brand and preparation are unknown. 8. Chinese local names provided as 素炒打抛 (simplified) and 素炒打拋 (traditional), representing a vegetarian stir-fried holy basil dish in Chinese. Malay local name 'Tumis Selasih Thai Vegetarian' provided as the closest natural Malay descriptor. Tamil local name சைவ தாய் வேப்பிலை வதக்கல் and Hindi local name शाकाहारी थाई तुलसी स्टर-फ्राई provided as descriptive equivalents for this Thai vegetarian dish served in Singapore. These multilingual names are descriptive translations as no established loanword exists in these languages for this specific dish.
